From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Inability to completely empty the bladder

Medical condition

| Urinary retention | |
| --- | --- |
| Other names | Ischuria, bladder failure, bladder obstruction |
|  | |

| [Symptoms](/wiki/Signs_and_symptoms "Signs and symptoms") | **Sudden onset**: Inability to urinate, low abdominal pain[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) **Long term**: Frequent urination, loss of bladder control, [urinary tract infection](/wiki/Urinary_tract_infection "Urinary tract infection")[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) |
| Types | Acute, chronic[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) |

| [Medication](/wiki/Medication "Medication") | [Alpha blockers](/wiki/Alpha_blockers "Alpha blockers") such as [terazosin](/wiki/Terazosin "Terazosin"), [5α-reductase inhibitors](/wiki/5%CE%B1-reductase_inhibitors "5α-reductase inhibitors") such as [finasteride](/wiki/Finasteride "Finasteride")[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) |

**Urinary retention** is an inability to completely empty the [bladder](/wiki/Bladder "Bladder").[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) Onset can be sudden or gradual.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) When of sudden onset, symptoms include an inability to urinate and lower abdominal pain.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) When of gradual onset, symptoms may include [loss of bladder control](/wiki/Urinary_incontinence "Urinary incontinence"), mild lower abdominal pain, and a weak urine stream.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) Those with long-term problems are at risk of [urinary tract infections](/wiki/Urinary_tract_infection "Urinary tract infection").[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1)

Causes include blockage of the [urethra](/wiki/Urethra "Urethra"), nerve problems, certain medications, and weak bladder muscles.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) Blockage can be caused by [benign prostatic hyperplasia](/wiki/Benign_prostatic_hyperplasia "Benign prostatic hyperplasia") (BPH), [urethral strictures](/wiki/Urethral_stricture "Urethral stricture"), [bladder stones](/wiki/Bladder_stones "Bladder stones"), a [cystocele](/wiki/Cystocele "Cystocele"), [constipation](/wiki/Constipation "Constipation"), or [tumors](/wiki/Tumors "Tumors").[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) Nerve problems can occur from [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes"), trauma, [spinal cord problems](/wiki/Spinal_cord_injury "Spinal cord injury"), [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), or [heavy metal poisoning](/wiki/Heavy_metal_poisoning "Heavy metal poisoning").[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) Medications that can cause problems include [anticholinergics](/wiki/Anticholinergic "Anticholinergic"), [antihistamines](/wiki/Antihistamines "Antihistamines"), [tricyclic antidepressants](/wiki/Tricyclic_antidepressants "Tricyclic antidepressants"), [cyclobenzaprine](/wiki/Cyclobenzaprine "Cyclobenzaprine"), [diazepam](/wiki/Diazepam "Diazepam"), [nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs](/wiki/Nonsteroidal_anti-inflammatory_drug "Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug") (NSAID), [stimulants](/wiki/Stimulants "Stimulants"), and [opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid").[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1)[[2]](#cite_note-acdj-2) Diagnosis is typically based on measuring the amount of urine in the bladder after urinating.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1)

Treatment is typically with a [catheter](/wiki/Urinary_catheterization "Urinary catheterization") either through the urethra or [lower abdomen](/wiki/Suprapubic_catheter "Suprapubic catheter").[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Other treatments may include medication to decrease the size of the prostate, [urethral dilatation](/wiki/Urethral_dilatation "Urethral dilatation"), a [urethral stent](/wiki/Urethral_stent "Urethral stent"), or surgery.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) Males are more often affected than females.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) In males over the age of 40 about 6 per 1,000 are affected a year.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) Among males over 80 this increases 30%.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1)

## Signs and symptoms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Signs and symptoms")]

Onset can be sudden or gradual.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) When the onset is sudden, symptoms include an inability to urinate and lower abdominal pain.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) When of gradual onset, symptoms may include [loss of bladder control](/wiki/Urinary_incontinence "Urinary incontinence"), mild lower abdominal pain, and a weak urine stream.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1) Those with long-term problems are at risk of [urinary tract infections](/wiki/Urinary_tract_infection "Urinary tract infection").[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1)

### Complications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Complications")]

The urinary bag of a person with post obstructive diuresis

Acute urinary retention is a [medical emergency](/wiki/Medical_emergency "Medical emergency") and requires prompt treatment. The pain can be excruciating when urine is not able to flow out. Moreover, one can develop severe [sweating](/wiki/Perspiration "Perspiration"), [chest pain](/wiki/Chest_pain "Chest pain"), [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety") and [high blood pressure](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension"). Other patients may develop a shock-like condition and may require admission to a hospital. Serious complications of untreated urinary retention include [bladder](/wiki/Urinary_bladder "Urinary bladder") damage and chronic [kidney failure](/wiki/Renal_failure "Renal failure").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Urinary retention is a disorder treated in a hospital, and the quicker one seeks treatment, the fewer the complications.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the longer term, obstruction of the urinary tract may cause:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Bladder stones](/wiki/Bladder_stone "Bladder stone")
* [Atrophy](/wiki/Atrophy "Atrophy") of the [detrusor muscle](/wiki/Detrusor_muscle "Detrusor muscle") (atonic bladder is an extreme form)
* [Hydronephrosis](/wiki/Hydronephrosis "Hydronephrosis") (congestion of the kidneys)
* [Hypertrophy](/wiki/Hypertrophy "Hypertrophy") of the [detrusor muscle](/wiki/Detrusor_muscle "Detrusor muscle") (the muscle that squeezes the bladder to empty it during urination)
* [Diverticula](/wiki/Diverticula "Diverticula") (formation of pouches) in the bladder wall (which can lead to stones and infection)

## Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Causes")]

### Bladder

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Bladder")]

* Infection[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* [Detrusor sphincter dyssynergia](/wiki/Bladder_sphincter_dyssynergia "Bladder sphincter dyssynergia")
* [Neurogenic bladder](/wiki/Neurogenic_bladder_dysfunction "Neurogenic bladder dysfunction") (commonly spinal cord damage, pelvic splanchnic nerve damage, cauda equina syndrome, pontine micturition or storage center lesions, [demyelinating diseases](/wiki/Demyelinating_diseases "Demyelinating diseases"), [multiple system atrophy](/wiki/Multiple_system_atrophy "Multiple system atrophy"), [genital herpes](/wiki/Genital_herpes "Genital herpes"), or [meningitis-retention syndrome](/wiki/Meningitis-retention_syndrome "Meningitis-retention syndrome"))
* [Iatrogenic](/wiki/Iatrogenesis "Iatrogenesis") (caused by medical treatment/procedure) scarring of the bladder neck (commonly from removal of indwelling [catheters](/wiki/Catheters "Catheters") or [cystoscopy](/wiki/Cystoscopy "Cystoscopy") operations)
* Damage to the bladder

### Prostate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Prostate")]

* [Benign prostatic hyperplasia](/wiki/Benign_prostatic_hyperplasia "Benign prostatic hyperplasia") (BPH)
* [Prostate cancer](/wiki/Prostate_cancer "Prostate cancer") and other pelvic [malignancies](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer")
* [Prostatitis](/wiki/Prostatitis "Prostatitis")

### Penile urethra

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Penile urethra")]

* [Congenital urethral valves](/wiki/Congenital_urethral_valves "Congenital urethral valves")
* [Phimosis](/wiki/Phimosis "Phimosis") or pinhole meatus
* [Circumcision](/wiki/Circumcision "Circumcision")
* Obstruction in the urethra, for example a stricture (usually caused either by injury or STD), a metastasis or a precipitated [pseudogout](/wiki/Pseudogout "Pseudogout") crystal in the urine
* [Pseudodyssynergia](/wiki/Pseudodyssynergia "Pseudodyssynergia")
* STD lesions ([gonorrhoea](/wiki/Gonorrhea "Gonorrhea") causes numerous strictures, leading to a "rosary bead" appearance, whereas chlamydia usually causes a single stricture)
* [Emasculation](/wiki/Emasculation "Emasculation")

### Postoperative

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Postoperative")]

Risk factors include

* Age: Older people may have [degeneration](/wiki/Degeneration_(medical) "Degeneration (medical)") of [neural pathways](/wiki/Neural_pathway "Neural pathway") involved with bladder function and it can lead to an increased risk of postoperative urinary retention.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) The risk of postoperative urinary retention increases up to 2.11 fold for people older than 60 years.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6)
* Medications: [Anticholinergics](/wiki/Anticholinergics "Anticholinergics") and medications with anticholinergic properties, [alpha-adrenergic agonists](/wiki/Alpha-adrenergic_agonist "Alpha-adrenergic agonist"), [opiates](/wiki/Opiate "Opiate"), nonsteroidal anti-inflammatories (NSAIDs), [calcium-channel blockers](/wiki/Calcium_channel_blocker "Calcium channel blocker") and [beta-adrenergic agonists](/wiki/Beta-adrenergic_agonist "Beta-adrenergic agonist"), may increase the risk.[[2]](#cite_note-acdj-2)[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6)
* Anesthesia: General anesthetics during surgery may cause bladder [atony](/wiki/Atony "Atony") by acting as a smooth muscle relaxant.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) General anesthetics can directly interfere with autonomic regulation of [detrusor](/wiki/Detrusor_muscle "Detrusor muscle") tone and predispose people to bladder overdistention and subsequent retention.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) Spinal anesthesia results in a blockade of the [micturition reflex](/wiki/Urination "Urination").[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) Spinal anesthesia shows a higher risk of postoperative urinary retention compared to general anesthesia.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6)
* [Benign prostatic hyperplasia](/wiki/Benign_prostatic_hyperplasia "Benign prostatic hyperplasia"): Men with benign prostatic hyperplasia are at an increased risk of acute urinary retention.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6)
* Surgery related: Operative times longer than 2 hours may lead to an increased risk of postoperative urinary retention 3-fold.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6)
* Postoperative pain.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6)

### Chronic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Chronic")]

Chronic urinary retention that is due to bladder blockage which can either be as a result of muscle damage or neurological damage.[[7]](#cite_note-Stof2017-7) If the retention is due to neurological damage, there is a disconnect between the brain to muscle communication, which can make it impossible to completely empty the bladder.[[7]](#cite_note-Stof2017-7) If the retention is due to muscle damage, it is likely that the muscles are not able to contract enough to completely empty the bladder.[[7]](#cite_note-Stof2017-7)

The most common cause of chronic urinary retention is BPH.[[1]](#cite_note-NIH2014-1)

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other")]

* [Tethered spinal cord syndrome](/wiki/Tethered_spinal_cord_syndrome "Tethered spinal cord syndrome").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Psychogenic](/wiki/Somatic_symptom_disorder "Somatic symptom disorder") causes – psychosocial stresses, fear associated with urination, [paruresis](/wiki/Paruresis "Paruresis") ("shy bladder syndrome") – in extreme cases, urinary retention can result.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [noradrenergic](/wiki/Noradrenergic "Noradrenergic") drugs, that includes [tricyclic antidepressants](/wiki/Tricyclic_antidepressants "Tricyclic antidepressants"), as well as [duloxetine](/wiki/Duloxetine "Duloxetine"), [reboxetine](/wiki/Reboxetine "Reboxetine"), [atomoxetine](/wiki/Atomoxetine "Atomoxetine"), [venlafaxine](/wiki/Venlafaxine "Venlafaxine"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) and [stimulants](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant"), such as [methylphenidate](/wiki/Methylphenidate "Methylphenidate"), [amphetamine](/wiki/Amphetamine "Amphetamine") and [MDMA](/wiki/Methylenedioxymethamphetamine "Methylenedioxymethamphetamine").
* Use of NSAIDs, or drugs with [anticholinergic](/wiki/Anticholinergic "Anticholinergic") properties.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Stones or metastases, which can theoretically appear anywhere along the urinary tract, but vary in frequency depending on anatomy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Muscarinic antagonists](/wiki/Muscarinic_antagonist "Muscarinic antagonist") such as [atropine](/wiki/Atropine "Atropine") and [scopolamine](/wiki/Hyoscine_hydrobromide "Hyoscine hydrobromide").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Malfunctioning [artificial urinary sphincter](/wiki/Artificial_urinary_sphincter "Artificial urinary sphincter").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Diagnosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Diagnosis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Urinaryobstruction.png/250px-Urinaryobstruction.png)](/wiki/File:Urinaryobstruction.png)

As seen on axial CT

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Ultrasound_of_trabeculated_urinary_bladder.jpg/250px-Ultrasound_of_trabeculated_urinary_bladder.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ultrasound_of_trabeculated_urinary_bladder.jpg)

[Ultrasonography](/wiki/Medical_ultrasonography "Medical ultrasonography") showing a trabeculated wall, seen as small irregularities mainly at left (superior part). This is strongly associated with urinary retention.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Analysis of urine flow may aid in establishing the type of [micturition](/wiki/Micturition "Micturition") (urination) abnormality. Common findings, determined by ultrasound of the bladder, include a slow rate of flow, intermittent flow, and a large amount of urine retained in the bladder after urination. A normal test result should be 20–25 [ml](/wiki/Milliliter "Milliliter")/s peak flow rate. A post-void residual urine greater than 50 ml is a significant amount of urine and increases the potential for recurring urinary tract infections.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In adults older than 60 years, 50-100 ml of residual urine may remain after each voiding because of the decreased contractility of the [detrusor muscle](/wiki/Detrusor_urinae_muscle "Detrusor urinae muscle").[[7]](#cite_note-Stof2017-7) In chronic retention, ultrasound of the bladder may show massive increase in bladder capacity (normal capacity is 400-600 ml).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Non-neurogenic chronic urinary retention does not have a standardized definition; however, urine volumes >300mL can be used as an informal indicator.[[7]](#cite_note-Stof2017-7) Diagnosis of urinary retention is conducted over a period of 6 months, with 2 separate measurements of urine volume 6 months apart. Measurements should have a PVR (post-void residual) volume of >300ml.[[7]](#cite_note-Stof2017-7)

Determining the serum [prostate-specific antigen](/wiki/Prostate-specific_antigen "Prostate-specific antigen") (PSA) may help diagnose or rule out prostate cancer, though this is also raised in BPH and [prostatitis](/wiki/Prostatitis "Prostatitis"). A [TRUS](/wiki/TRUS "TRUS") biopsy of the prostate (transrectal ultrasound guided) can distinguish between these prostate conditions. Serum [urea](/wiki/Urea "Urea") and [creatinine](/wiki/Creatinine "Creatinine") determinations may be necessary to rule out backflow kidney damage. [Cystoscopy](/wiki/Cystoscopy "Cystoscopy") may be needed to explore the urinary passage and rule out blockages.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In acute cases of urinary retention where associated symptoms in the lumbar spine are present such as pain, numbness ([saddle anesthesia](/wiki/Saddle_anesthesia "Saddle anesthesia")), parasthesias, decreased anal sphincter tone, or altered deep tendon reflexes, an MRI of the lumbar spine should be considered to further assess [cauda equina syndrome](/wiki/Cauda_equina_syndrome "Cauda equina syndrome").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Treatment")]

In acute urinary retention, [urinary catheterization](/wiki/Urinary_catheterization "Urinary catheterization"), placement of a [prostatic stent](/wiki/Prostatic_stent "Prostatic stent"), or [suprapubic cystostomy](/wiki/Suprapubic_cystostomy "Suprapubic cystostomy") relieves the retention. In the longer term, treatment depends on the cause. BPH may respond to [alpha blocker](/wiki/Alpha_blocker "Alpha blocker") and [5-alpha-reductase inhibitor](/wiki/5-alpha-reductase_inhibitor "5-alpha-reductase inhibitor") therapy, or surgically with [prostatectomy](/wiki/Prostatectomy "Prostatectomy") or [transurethral resection of the prostate](/wiki/Transurethral_resection_of_the_prostate "Transurethral resection of the prostate") (TURP).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Use of alpha-blockers can provide relief of urinary retention following de-catheterization for both men and women.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) In case, if catheter can't be negotiated, suprapubic puncture can be done with lumbar puncture needle.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Medication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Medication")]

#### α1-receptor antagonists and 5α-reductase inhibitors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: α1-receptor antagonists and 5α-reductase inhibitors")]

Urinary retention, including drug-induced cases, may be an early sign of [benign prostatic hyperplasia](/wiki/Benign_prostatic_hyperplasia "Benign prostatic hyperplasia") (BPH). Treatment typically includes [α1-receptor antagonists](/wiki/Alpha-1_blocker "Alpha-1 blocker") such as [tamsulosin](/wiki/Tamsulosin "Tamsulosin"), which relaxes [smooth muscle in the bladder neck](/wiki/Internal_urethral_sphincter "Internal urethral sphincter"), and [5α-reductase inhibitors](/wiki/5%CE%B1-reductase_inhibitor "5α-reductase inhibitor") like [finasteride](/wiki/Finasteride "Finasteride") and [dutasteride](/wiki/Dutasteride "Dutasteride"), which reduce prostate enlargement. Clinical trials have demonstrated that combining these medications improves urinary symptoms and lowers the likelihood of retention recurrence.[[13]](#cite_note-Verhamme2008-13)

#### Striated muscle relaxants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Striated muscle relaxants")]

[Baclofen](/wiki/Baclofen "Baclofen"), a [gamma-aminobutyric acid](/wiki/Gamma-aminobutyric_acid "Gamma-aminobutyric acid") (GABA) agonist, acts on GABAergic [interneurons](/wiki/Interneuron "Interneuron") in the [sacral](/wiki/Sacrum "Sacrum") [intermediolateral cell column](/wiki/Intermediolateral_nucleus "Intermediolateral nucleus"), facilitating the relaxation of the [striated urinary sphincter](/wiki/External_sphincter_muscle_of_urethra_(disambiguation) "External sphincter muscle of urethra (disambiguation)") during voiding. Some evidence suggests it may be beneficial for women with [bladder outlet obstruction](/wiki/Bladder_outlet_obstruction "Bladder outlet obstruction")[[14]](#cite_note-EAU2024-14) and pediatric patients.[[15]](#cite_note-Tousignant2025-15)

#### Opioid antagonists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Opioid antagonists")]

[Naloxone](/wiki/Naloxone "Naloxone"), has been tested for urinary retention following [epidural](/wiki/Epidural "Epidural") or [intrathecal](/wiki/Intrathecal "Intrathecal") anesthesia. While effective, it also reverses [analgesia](/wiki/Analgesia "Analgesia"), making it unsuitable for postoperative cases. [Nalbuphine](/wiki/Nalbuphine "Nalbuphine"), a [mixed agonist/antagonist](/wiki/Agonist-antagonist "Agonist-antagonist") [opioid modulator](/wiki/Opioid_modulator "Opioid modulator"), has shown promise in a reported case of postoperative urinary retention, preserving analgesia while relieving retention. Further studies are needed to confirm its efficacy.[[13]](#cite_note-Verhamme2008-13)

### Pelvic floor muscle training and biofeedback

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Pelvic floor muscle training and biofeedback")]

[Pelvic floor muscle training](/wiki/Kegel_exercise "Kegel exercise") (PFMT), sometimes combined with [biofeedback](/wiki/Biofeedback "Biofeedback"), is a treatment that aim to teach patients to relax their pelvic floor muscles (PFMs) and [striated urinary sphincter](/wiki/External_sphincter_muscle_of_urethra_(disambiguation) "External sphincter muscle of urethra (disambiguation)") during voiding. It has been shown that pelvic floor muscle contraction, especially in women with pelvic floor dysfunction, can significantly reduce vaginal resting pressure and surface [electromyography](/wiki/Electromyography "Electromyography") (EMG) activity. However, the majority of evidence supporting PFMT for dysfunctional voiding comes from studies of children, not adults.[[14]](#cite_note-EAU2024-14)

### Electrical stimulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Electrical stimulation")]

Electrical stimulation, or [neuromodulation](/wiki/Neuromodulation "Neuromodulation") involves the application of [electrodes](/wiki/Electrodes "Electrodes") to induce controlled contraction and relaxation of the pelvic floor muscles. The goal of this intervention is to facilitate relaxation of the [external sphincter](/wiki/External_sphincter_muscle_of_urethra_(disambiguation) "External sphincter muscle of urethra (disambiguation)") and [pelvic floor](/wiki/Pelvic_floor "Pelvic floor") muscles, which may assist in voiding.[[14]](#cite_note-EAU2024-14) Different methods of electrical stimulation are used, including:

* [Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation](/wiki/Transcutaneous_electrical_nerve_stimulation "Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation") (TENS): This [FDA](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration")-approved method involves applying electrodes to the skin. TENS has been studied as a treatment for [idiopathic](/wiki/Idiopathic "Idiopathic") non-obstructive urinary retention (NOUR).[[16]](#cite_note-Groat2015-16) Studies show that TENS can be applied at different locations, such as transvaginally, over the [symphysis pubis](/wiki/Pubic_symphysis "Pubic symphysis") and [ischial tuberosity](/wiki/Ischial_tuberosity "Ischial tuberosity")[[17]](#cite_note-Coolen2021-17) to stimulate the [pudendal nerve](/wiki/Pudendal_nerve "Pudendal nerve"),[[18]](#cite_note-Jezernik2002-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Grill2009-19) or at the [second sacral foramina](/wiki/Sacral_spinal_nerve_2 "Sacral spinal nerve 2") and lower abdomen.[[17]](#cite_note-Coolen2021-17)

:   In adults with idiopathic NOUR, transvaginal TENS has demonstrated a subjective success rate of 80% with patients reporting a stronger stream when voiding. Another study showed that TENS applied to the sacral foramina and lower abdomen combined with conservative treatment resulted in a 43% decrease in the number of patients relying on catheterization.[[17]](#cite_note-Coolen2021-17)
:   In children with idiopathic NOUR, TENS combined with [pelvic floor muscle training](/wiki/Kegel_exercise "Kegel exercise") reduced postvoid residual and urinary tract infections.[[17]](#cite_note-Coolen2021-17)

* [Percutaneous tibial nerve stimulation](/wiki/Percutaneous_tibial_nerve_stimulation "Percutaneous tibial nerve stimulation") (PTNS): This minimally invasive [FDA](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration")-approved method involves placing a needle electrode near the tibial nerve.[[16]](#cite_note-Groat2015-16) The objective success rate of PTNS (defined as a 50% or greater decrease in catheterizations or catheterized volume) ranges from 25% to 41%. Subjective success rates, based on patient's desire to continue treatment, range from 46.7% to 59%.[[17]](#cite_note-Coolen2021-17)
* Implantable neuromodulator devices: An implantable device is used to [stimulate the sacral nerves](/wiki/Sacral_nerve_stimulation "Sacral nerve stimulation") leading to the contraction of the [detrusor muscle](/wiki/Detrusor_muscle "Detrusor muscle"). This technique can be combined with [posterior rhizotomy](/wiki/Selective_dorsal_rhizotomy "Selective dorsal rhizotomy") to reduce detrusor hyperreflexia.[[18]](#cite_note-Jezernik2002-18)

### Extracorporeal magnetic stimulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Extracorporeal magnetic stimulation")]

Extracorporeal magnetic stimulation is a non-invasive method that involves the patient sitting on a device that induces consistent [pelvic floor](/wiki/Pelvic_floor "Pelvic floor") muscles contraction and relaxation at a set frequency and interval by repeated magnetic stimulation of motor nerves.[[14]](#cite_note-EAU2024-14)

### Catheter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Catheter")]

Acute urinary retention is treated by placement of a urinary catheter (small thin flexible tube) into the bladder. This can be either an [intermittent catheter](/wiki/Intermittent_catheter "Intermittent catheter") or a [Foley catheter](/wiki/Foley_catheter "Foley catheter") that is placed with a small inflatable bulb that holds the catheter in place.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Intermittent catheterization can be done by a health care professional or by the person themselves (clean intermittent self catheterization). Intermittent catheterization performed at the hospital is a sterile technique. Patients can be taught to use a [self catheterization](/wiki/Self_catheterization "Self catheterization") technique in one simple demonstration,[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20) and that reduces the rate of infection from long-term Foley catheters. Self catheterization requires doing the procedure periodically during the day, the frequency depending on fluid intake and bladder capacity. If fluid intake/outflow is around 1.5 litres per day, this would typically be performed roughly three times per day, i.e. roughly every six to eight hours during the day, more frequently when fluid intake is higher and/or bladder capacity lower.

For acute urinary retention, treatment requires urgent placement of a urinary catheter. A permanent urinary catheter may cause discomfort and pain that can last several days.

Older people with ongoing problems may require continued intermittent self catheterization (CISC). CISC has a lower infection risk compared to catheterization techniques that stay within the body.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20)

Challenges with CISC include compliance issues as some people may not be able to place the catheter themselves.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Surgery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Surgery")]

The [chronic](/wiki/Chronic_(medicine) "Chronic (medicine)") form of urinary retention may require some type of [surgical](/wiki/Surgery "Surgery") procedure. While both procedures are relatively safe, complications can occur.

In most patients with [benign prostate hyperplasia](/wiki/Benign_prostate_hyperplasia "Benign prostate hyperplasia") (BPH), a procedure known as [transurethral resection of the prostate](/wiki/Transurethral_resection_of_the_prostate "Transurethral resection of the prostate") (TURP) may be performed to relieve bladder obstruction.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Surgical complications from TURP include a bladder infection, bleeding from the prostate, scar formation, inability to hold urine, and inability to have an erection. The majority of these complications are short lived, and most individuals recover fully within 6–12 months.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Sitting voiding position

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Sitting voiding position")]

A [meta-analysis](/wiki/Meta-analysis "Meta-analysis") on the influence of voiding position on [urodynamics](/wiki/Urodynamics "Urodynamics") in males with [lower urinary tract symptoms](/wiki/Lower_urinary_tract_symptoms "Lower urinary tract symptoms") showed that in the sitting position, the residual urine in the bladder was significantly reduced, the maximum urinary flow was increased, and the voiding time was decreased. For healthy males, no influence was found on these parameters, meaning that they can urinate in either position.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Epidemiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urinary_retention&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Epidemiology")]

Urinary retention is a common disorder in elderly males. The most common cause of urinary retention is BPH. This disorder starts around age 50 and symptoms may appear after 10–15 years. BPH is a progressive disorder and narrows the neck of the bladder leading to urinary retention. By the age of 70, almost 10 percent of males have some degree of BPH and 33% have it by the eighth decade of life. While BPH rarely causes sudden urinary retention, the condition can become acute in the presence of certain medications including [antihypertensives](/wiki/Antihypertensive "Antihypertensive"), [antihistamines](/wiki/Antihistamine "Antihistamine"), and [antiparkinson](/wiki/Management_of_Parkinson%27s_disease#Medication "Management of Parkinson's disease") medications, and after [spinal anaesthesia](/wiki/Spinal_anaesthesia "Spinal anaesthesia") or [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In young males, the most common cause of urinary retention is infection of the prostate (*acute [prostatitis](/wiki/Prostatitis "Prostatitis")*). The infection is acquired during sexual intercourse and presents with low back pain, penile discharge, low grade [fever](/wiki/Fever "Fever") and an inability to pass urine. The exact number of individuals with acute prostatitis is unknown, because many do not seek treatment. In the US, at least 1–3 percent of males under the age of 40 develop urinary difficulty as a result of acute prostatitis. Most physicians and other health care professionals are aware of these disorders. Worldwide, both BPH and acute prostatitis have been found in males of all races and ethnic backgrounds. Cancers of the urinary tract can cause urinary obstruction but the process is more gradual. [Cancer of the bladder](/wiki/Bladder_cancer "Bladder cancer"), [prostate](/wiki/Prostate_cancer "Prostate cancer") or [ureters](/wiki/Ureter_cancer "Ureter cancer") can gradually obstruct urine output. Cancers often present with [blood in the urine](/wiki/Hematuria "Hematuria"), [weight loss](/wiki/Weight_loss "Weight loss"), lower back pain or gradual distension in the flanks.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Urinary retention in females is uncommon, occurring 1 in 100,000 every year, with a female-to-male incidence rate of 1:13. It is usually transient. The causes of UR in women can be multi-factorial, and can be postoperative and postpartum. Prompt urethral catheterization usually resolves the problem.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
